translation.en: She seems to have left already.

notes_md:
**dường như** = “seem” or “it seems that.” It shows you’re not 100% sure, just giving an impression.

It usually goes before a full clause.  

It makes your statement softer and less direct than a plain statement.  
Close to **có vẻ (như)** — both mean “seem,” but *dường như* feels a bit more thoughtful or reflective.
exampleSentencesTranslated: He seem not to know there is an important meeting today.
You seem to have forgotten to bring the keys when leaving the house.
It seem like it will rain, so she brought an umbrella just in case.

example.en: It seems like it's going to rain.

content.sentence: Cậu ấy ___ không vui hôm nay.

exampleSentences: Dường như anh ấy không biết hôm nay có cuộc họp quan trọng.
Em dường như đã quên mang theo chìa khóa khi ra khỏi nhà.
Trời dường như sẽ mưa nên chị mang theo ô để phòng.
notes:
dường như = “seem” or “it seems that.” It shows you’re not 100% sure, just giving an impression.
